flashcards.front: What is motion?
flashcards.back: Motion is the change in position of an object with respect to time and a reference point.

flashcards.front: What is displacement?
flashcards.back: The shortest distance measured from the initial to the final position of an object in a specific direction.

flashcards.front: What is distance?
flashcards.back: The total path length covered by an object, irrespective of direction.

flashcards.front: Can displacement be zero while distance is not?
flashcards.back: Yes, for example when an object returns to its starting point, displacement is zero but distance is non-zero.

flashcards.front: What is uniform motion?
flashcards.back: When an object covers equal distances in equal intervals of time.

flashcards.front: What is non-uniform motion?
flashcards.back: When an object covers unequal distances in equal intervals of time.

flashcards.front: Define speed.
flashcards.back: The distance travelled by an object per unit time.

flashcards.front: Define velocity.
flashcards.back: The displacement of an object per unit time in a given direction.

flashcards.front: What is average speed?
flashcards.back: Total distance travelled divided by total time taken.

flashcards.front: What is average velocity?
flashcards.back: Total displacement divided by total time taken.

flashcards.front: What is acceleration?
flashcards.back: The rate of change of velocity per unit time.

flashcards.front: What is uniform acceleration?
flashcards.back: When velocity changes by equal amounts in equal intervals of time.

flashcards.front: What is non-uniform acceleration?
flashcards.back: When velocity changes by unequal amounts in equal intervals of time.

flashcards.front: What is the SI unit of speed and velocity?
flashcards.back: Metre per second (m/s).

flashcards.front: What is the SI unit of acceleration?
flashcards.back: Metre per second squared (m/s²).

flashcards.front: State the three equations of motion.
flashcards.back: v = u + at; s = ut + ½at²; v² - u² = 2as.

flashcards.front: What does the slope of a distance-time graph represent?
flashcards.back: Speed of the object.

flashcards.front: What does the slope of a velocity-time graph represent?
flashcards.back: Acceleration of the object.

flashcards.front: What does the area under a velocity-time graph represent?
flashcards.back: Displacement of the object.

flashcards.front: Why does a passenger in a moving bus see roadside trees moving backwards?
flashcards.back: Because motion is observed relative to a reference point; the passenger’s frame of reference differs from that of a person standing outside.

flashcards.front: Why does the odometer of a car measure distance but not displacement?
flashcards.back: Because it records the total path length travelled, irrespective of direction.

flashcards.front: Why is Usha’s average velocity zero when she swims back and forth in a pool?
flashcards.back: Because her final position coincides with her initial position, making displacement zero.

flashcards.front: Why do drivers need to maintain distance between vehicles?
flashcards.back: Because braking produces negative acceleration, and the car still travels some distance before stopping.

flashcards.front: Why does thunder reach us after lightning?
flashcards.back: Because light travels much faster than sound, so we see lightning instantly but hear thunder later.

flashcards.front: Why is the motion of a freely falling body considered uniformly accelerated?
flashcards.back: Because its velocity increases by equal amounts in equal intervals of time due to gravity.
